author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: the sludge is a mix of both - once you have cleaned it the GO will dissolve in water and the graphite will not. Dissolve it in clean DI water and this time keep the solution that is GO - of course there is a lot of waste here and yield is low you are throwing a lot of GO away in the earlier washing processes - but as I said - it is a home experiment and producing any GO is an achievement.

author: @harikrishnanjl
text: So all the sediment at the bottom....the thick stuff. Is that the grapene oxide.or unreacted graphite? How do we separate the two? We are interested in the gr oxide. I am wondering would we lose the Graphene oxide when we discard the supernatant after we wash it with H2O2. If we centrifuge it won't the unreacted graphite oxide (the thick stuff i guess) and graphene oxide  be together? Any idea how to separate them if possible?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: It's a homemade drill centrifuge! - as fast as it will go until most of the brown stuff is at the bottom. Chuck the water away if you want or keep it for a few days and let the oxide settle. What can I say this is a home experiment. You need to use common sense and a bit of patience to get it to work.

author: @harikrishnanjl
text:
like in this paper (Silicon Nanoparticles-Graphene Paper Composites for Li Ion Battery Anodes-Supporting Information) it mentions decant the supernatant after H2O2 addition. 

Then in your video it says add DI water and then centrifuge. Then add more water & centrifuge it. After each step what r we interested in? supernatant or sediment?

author: @harikrishnanjl
text:
Just what I was looking for :-)
1) At 8 mins, when u add H2O2 u said centrifuge it (at what RPM)!!
2) Should we discard the supernatant solution or the bottom precipitate
3) Same goes after subsequent washing steps .....coz u mentioned it was graphite at bottom & Graphene oxide in supernatant soln?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Hiya mate - I have been away for the past week and out of touch with the internet - so, sorry for the lateness of my reply - But - Lots will work, pottasium permanganate being favoured but you can also use hydrogen peroxide, perchlorates, and nitrates - quite a few things.

author: @MrGoatflakes
text: can you just use nitric acid? Do other oxidisers work?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: You can use pretty much anything - polar or non polar - water, ethanol, PVP, toluene - tons of stuff - but it doesn't dissolve. You are not going to escape sonicating it. This is the problem i am facing at the moment and have been having a great deal of problems with - luckily a friend of mine came up with a solution - it will disperse in a cheap sonic bath and these are around $30 - check out his channel for exact details Eric Goeken he has a couple of vids where he does exactly what you want

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Urethane reducer? - that's basically a paint thinner isn't it? I am not sure why you want to do that. If it was to act as a carrier for the graphene oxide it wouldn't really add anything - well it would dry quicker - but the graphene oxide won't disperse any better in it. The problem here is one of controlling the sheet. If you dry sprinkle it then you will probably get little to no connectivity between the particles and so no conductivity. Your large panel idea is brilliant.

author: @geauxracerx
text:
Urethane reducer is composed of

n-Butyl Acetate
Ethyl Acetate
 Toluene 
Acetate

author: @geauxracerx
text:
50 square meters wow..

Hmm.... How do you think it would effect it if it was then mixed with some Urethane reducer? 
Do you think it would make it able to hold more charge if a few coats "Layers" (offering more surface area) were properly applied..
I say properly. I'm guessing a smooth coat, and a dry dusting... You could spray it on huge panels like the size of a solar panel.. Man a few layers that size could hold so much juice.. Don't you think?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: Certainly, but when you dry it you have to do so at a low temperature to have an even drying. I am not sure about the UV light option though I have heard rumors of people doing this but with limited success. If you have access to a large xenon lamp it should flash reduce.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: About 5 grams - a lot! the solubility is 4mg per ml you can make just over a litre of the oxide in solution and you are coating your substrate very thinly so a little will go a long way - my guess is this will coat a surface to the order of 50 square meters

author: @geauxracerx
text: I was also wondering if perhaps a Pyrex baking pan were used could the Graphene perhaps be made into much larger sheets by placing in an oven, or exposing to UV light?

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
3) Microwave assisted exfoliation and reduction of graphite oxide for ultra capacitors Zhu, Murali et al, Carbon (2010) doi: 10.1016/j.carbon.2010.02.001
4) layer structured graphite oxide as a novel adsorbent for humic acid removal from aqueous solution, Hartono, Wang et al' Journal of colloid and interface science 333(2009) 114-119 doi: 10.1016/j.cis.2009.02.005
Hope this helps

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text:
I used quite a few - but the four most important were: 
1) Fabrication and characterization of graphene hydrogel via hydrothermal approach as a scaffold for preliminary study of cell growth, Lim, HUang, et al International journal of nanomedicine 2011:6 1817-1823
2) Simple room-temperature preparation of high-yield large-area graphene oxide, lim ,huang, et al, International journal of nanomedicine 2011:6 3443-3448
